{"step_by_step_thinking": "Methotrexate is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) commonly used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Multiple documents from the MEDCORP source confirm that methotrexate is the most commonly used agent for initial treatment of RA. Document [1] states that methotrexate is the most commonly used agent as an initial treatment. Document [2] specifies that methotrexate is the preferred agent for initial monotherapy in DMARD-naive patients with RA. Document [10] confirms methotrexate as the initial drug of choice, with a recommended starting dose of 10-15 mg/week. Document [19] and [23] further support that methotrexate monotherapy is strongly recommended over other therapies in DMARD-naive patients with moderate-to-high disease activity. Additionally, Document [20] notes that tofacitinib can be used in sequence with methotrexate, indicating methotrexate is part of standard treatment pathways. The UMLS documents do not contradict this but instead classify methotrexate within broader antirheumatic and anti-inflammatory drug categories, which aligns with its established use. Therefore, all evidence supports that methotrexate is used for the treatment of RA.", "answer_choice": "A"}
